founding_assumptions: Teams need a faster collaboration layer than email.
A free entry point can drive bottoms-up adoption.
key_decisions: Repositioned an internal tool as the primary product.
Focused distribution on collaborative teams with many integrations.
major_pivots: Pivoted from Tiny Speck's game business into workplace communication software.
go_to_market_approach: Product-led growth with a free tier and team-based viral adoption.
what_worked: Internal collaboration pain was acute and easy to demonstrate.
Integrations and team invitations reinforced retention.
what_failed: Early enterprise competition was stronger than initially assumed.
why_succeeded_or_failed: Slack succeeded because it pivoted into a concrete team communication problem, used product-led distribution, and benefited from strong collaborative network effects.
summary: A pivoted internal collaboration tool that found product-market fit with team-based adoption.

source_materials.title: Slack company history and product overview
source_materials.notes: Company-controlled source; growth framing may be optimistic.

evidence.excerpt: Slack originated as an internal communication tool built by Tiny Speck before becoming its main product.
evidence.rationale: Supports the major pivot and origin story.

evidence.excerpt: Slack's growth is commonly described through team invites, integrations, and free-to-paid expansion.

uncertainty_notes: Timeline is rounded from public launch narratives.
Competitive pressure is synthesized from widely cited summaries rather than a single primary source.

extraction_notes: Seed example for curated dataset structure.

founding_assumptions: Real-identity social networking would spread rapidly through friend graphs.
Advertising could monetize a large consumer audience.
key_decisions: Prioritized rapid user growth ahead of infrastructure resilience.
Delayed major strategic response while newer rivals improved performance.
major_pivots: Attempted to reposition toward gaming after social-network leadership was lost.
go_to_market_approach: Viral consumer adoption through friend invitations and social graph growth.
pricing_strategy: Free product supported primarily by advertising expectations.
what_worked: The early social graph concept drove strong initial user growth.
what_failed: Frequent performance and uptime problems eroded user trust.
Competitive response lagged while Facebook improved product quality.
failure_modes: Product quality decline accelerated user churn.
why_succeeded_or_failed: Friendster failed to sustain leadership because technical reliability and competitive execution did not keep pace with demand, undermining the network effects that initially drove adoption.
summary: An early social-network leader whose growth outpaced infrastructure and competitive execution.

source_materials.title: Friendster historical summary
source_materials.notes: Secondary source; should be supplemented with deeper primary research before production use.

evidence.excerpt: Friendster struggled with speed and reliability issues during rapid growth.
The company later shifted focus toward gaming after losing momentum as a social network.

uncertainty_notes: Revenue-model specifics are simplified because public summaries emphasize growth and infrastructure more than monetization.
The failure narrative combines multiple retrospective summaries.

extraction_notes: Seed example for curated dataset structure.
